concepts.title: Equivalent Weight
concepts.conceptBody:
Equivalent Weight Concept

Equivalent Weight: Number of parts by mass of an element which reacts or displaces from a compound 1.008 parts by mass of hydrogen, 8 parts by mass of oxygen and 35.5 parts by mass of chlorine, is known as the equivalent weight of that element e.g.

(1)

$\begin{matrix} 2Mg & + & {{O}_{2}} & \to & 2MgO \\ 48g & {} & 32g & {} & {} \\ 12g & {} & 8g & {} & {} \\ \end{matrix}$
$\therefore $ $32~\text{g}$ of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ reacts with $48~\text{g}$ of $\text{Mg}$
$\therefore $ $8~\text{g}$ of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}=\frac{48\times 8}{32}=12~\text{g}$
$\therefore \quad $ Equivalent weight of $\text{Mg}=12$
Similarly, $\text{Zn}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\to \text{ZnS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}$
              $65.5~\text{g}$ $1.00~\text{g}$
$\therefore \quad 1.008~\text{g}$ of hydrogen is displaced by $65.5~\text{g}$
$\therefore \quad 1.008~\text{g}$ of $\text{H}=\frac{65.5}{2\times 1.008}=32.75~\text{g}$
$\therefore \quad \text{ Equivalent weight of Zn}=\frac{65.5}{2}=32.75$
(2) $\text{Al}+\frac{3}{2}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{AlC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$
$32~\text{g}\frac{3}{2}\quad 71~\text{g}\therefore 111.5~\text{g}$ chlorine reacts with $27~\text{g}$ of $\text{Al}$
$\therefore \quad 35.5~\text{g}$ chlorine reacts with $\frac{27\times 35.5}{111.5}=9.0~\text{g}$ of Al
$\therefore \quad $ Equivalent weight of aluminium $=\frac{27}{3}=9.0$
As we can see from the above examples that equivalent weight is the ratio of atomic weight and a factor (say n-factor) which is in above three cases is their respective valencies. So,
equivalent weight \(=\frac{\text { atomic weight }}{n \text {-factor }}\)
In a similar way, the equivalent weight of acid/base is the ratio of molecular weight and the basicity/acidity and for oxidizing agents and reducing agent it is the ratio of molecular weight and the number of moles of electrons gained or lost. So in case of acid/base the n-factor is basicity/acidity (i.e. number of dissociable ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ ions/number of dissociable $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ ion and in case of oxidizing agent/ reducing agent, $\text{n}$-factor is number of moles of electrons gained/lost per mole of oxidizing agent/ reducing agent. Therefore, in general, we can write.
$\text{Equivalent weight }(E)\text{ }=\frac{\text{ Atomic or molecular weight }}{\text{n}-\text{ factor }}\text{ }$
$\text{No}\text{. of equivalents of solute }=\frac{\text{Wt}}{\text{Eq}.\text{wt}.}=\frac{\text{W}}{\text{E}}=\frac{\text{W}}{\text{M}/\text{n}}$
\(\begin{aligned} & \text { No. of equivalents of solute }=\frac{\text { No. of moles of solute }}{\text { n-factor }} \\ & \text { And also Normality }=\frac{\text { n-factor }}{\text { molarity of solution }}\end{aligned}\)

concepts.title: Non-Redox reactions
concepts.conceptBody:
n-Factor Calculation

In Non-Redox Reaction

1. Acids

For acids, $n$-factor is defined as the number of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions replaced by 1 mole of acid in a reaction.

Note: That the n-factor for acid is not equal to its basicity; i.e. the number of moles of replaceable ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$atoms present in one mole of acid.

For example, n-factor of $\text{HCl}=1$,

n-factor of $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=1$,

n-factor of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}=1$ or 2 ,

depending upon extent of reaction it undergoes.

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{NaOH}\to \text{NaHS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Although one mole of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ has 2 replaceable $\text{H}$ atoms but in this reaction ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ has given only one ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ion, so its $\text{n}$-factor would be 1 .

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2\text{NaOH}\to \text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

The n-factor of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ in this reaction would be 2.

Similarly,

$\text{n-factor of }{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=1\text{ or }2$

$\text{n-factor of }{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=1\text{ or }2$

$\text{n-factor of }{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}=1\text{ or }2\text{ or }3$

n-factor of H 3 PO 3 = 1 or 2 or 2 because one of the $\text{H}$ is not replaceable in ${{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$.

This can be seen using its structure. This can be seen using its structure The$\text{H}$ atoms which are linked to oxygen are replaceable while the $\text{H}$ atom linked directly to central atom $(\text{P})$ is non replaceable.

n-factor of ${{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=1$

In ${{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$, although all three $\text{H}$ are linked to oxygen, yet all $3\text{H}$ are not replaceable. Here boron atom is electron deficient, so its acts as a Lewis acid. When ${{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ oxygen atom of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ through its lone pair attack the boron atom, as follows

The net reaction is ${{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\to {{\left[ \text{B}{{(\text{OH})}_{4}} \right]}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$

Thus, one mole of ${{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{B}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ in solution gives only one mole of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$, so its $\text{n}$-factor is 1 .

2. Bases

For bases, $\text{n}$-factor is defined as the number of $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ions replaced by 1 mole of base in a reaction. Note that n-factor is not equal to its acidity i.e. the number of moles of replaceable $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$ions present in 1 mole of base.

For example, $\text{n-factor of NaOH}=1\text{ }$

$\text{n-factor of Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}=1\text{ or }2$

$\text{n-factor of Al}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}}=1\text{ or }2\text{ or }3\text{ }$

$\text{n-factor of N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}(\text{OH})=1$

concepts.title: Redox reactions
concepts.conceptBody:
In Redox Reactions

1. Salts in which no atom undergoes change in oxidation state in reactions 

The $\text{n}$-factor for such salts is defined as the total moles of cationic/anionic charge replaced in 1 mole of the salt. For the reaction,

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+\text{BaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\to \text{NaCl}+\text{B}{{\text{a}}_{3}}{{\left( \text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}$

To get one mole of $\text{B}{{\text{a}}_{3}}{{\left( \text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}$, two moles of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ are required, which means six moles of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$ are completely replaced by 3 moles of $\text{B}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}$ ions. So, six moles of cationic charge is replaced by 2 moles of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$, thus each mole of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ replaces 3 moles of cationic charge. Hence, $\text{n}$-factor of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ in this reaction is 3 .

concepts.title: n-Factor for Salts
concepts.conceptBody:
N Factor of Salts

2. Salts which react in a manner that only one atom undergoes change in oxidation state and goes in only one product 

The $\text{n}$-factor of such salts is defined as the number of moles of electrons exchanged (lost or gained) by one mole of the salt.

Let us have a salt ${{\text{A}}_{\text{a}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{b}}}$ in which oxidation state of $\text{A}$ is $+\text{x}$. It changes to a compound, which has atom $\text{D}$ in it. The oxidation state of $\text{A}$ in ${{\text{A}}_{\text{c}}}\text{D}$ be $+\text{y}$.

$\text{A}_{\text{a}}^{+\text{x}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{b}}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}_{\text{c}}^{+\text{y}}\text{D}$

The $n$-factor of ${{A}_{a}}{{B}_{b}}$ is calculated as

$\text{n}=|\text{ax}-\text{ay}|$

To calculate the $\text{n-factor}$ of such salts, we apply following method:

1. Take one mole of reactant whose oxidation state is changing.

2. Multiply this with oxidation state of element in reactant to give us total oxidation state of element in reactant.

3. Calculate total oxidation state of the same element in the product for same number of moles of atom in reactant.

Another similar case would be that the salt has only one atom undergoing change in oxidation state but going into two different products with the same oxidation state. Try it yourself.

For example, let us calculate the $\text{n}$-factor $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ for the given chemical change.

$\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{{H}^{+}}}\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$

In this reaction, oxidation state of $\text{Mn}$ changes from +7 to +2 . Thus; $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is acting as oxidising agent, since it is reduced.

$\therefore \quad \text{n-factor of KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}=11\quad (+7)-1\quad (+2)=5.$

Similarly

(a) $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{{H}_{2}}O}\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+4}}$

$\text{n-factor of KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}=11\quad (+7)-1\quad (+4)=3$

(b) $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{O{{H}^{-}}}\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+6}}$

$\text{n-factor of KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}=\text{I}\quad (+7)-1\quad (+6)\text{I}=1$

It can be seen that in all the above chemical changes, $\text{KMn}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is acting as oxidising agent, yet its $n$-factor is not same in all reactions. Thus, the n-factor of a compound is not fixed, it depends on the type and the extent of reaction it undergoes.

Example 14

Calculate the n-factor of reactants in the given chemical changes?

(a) ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}\xrightarrow{{{H}^{+}}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{3+}}$

(b) ${{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{O}_{4}^{2-}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

(c) ${{\text{S}}_{2}}\text{O}_{3}^{2-}\xrightarrow{alkaline}\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}$

(d) ${{\text{I}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{ ICl }$

Solution. 

(a) ${{\text{K}}_{2}}\overset{+6}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}}}\,{{\text{O}}_{7}}\xrightarrow{{{H}^{+}}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}^{3+}}$

The oxidation state of $\text{Cr}$ changes from +6 to +3 .

$\therefore \quad \text{n}=12\quad (+6)-2\quad (+3)\mid =6$

(b) $\overset{+3}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{2}}}}\,\text{O}_{4}^{2-}\xrightarrow{{}}\overset{+4}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}\,$

Carbon get oxidized from +3 to +4

$\therefore \quad \text{n}=12\quad (+3)-2\quad (+4)\mid =2$

(c) $\overset{+2}{\mathop{~{{\text{S}}_{2}}}}\,\text{O}_{3}^{2-}\xrightarrow{alkaline}\overset{+6}{\mathop{~\text{S}}}\,\text{O}_{4}^{2-}$

The oxidation state of sulphur changes from +2 to +6

$\therefore \quad \text{n}=12\quad (+2)-2\quad (+6)\mid =8$

(d) ${{\text{I}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\overset{+1}{\mathop{\text{I}Cl}}\,$

${{\text{I}}^{-}}$get oxidized to ${{\text{I}}^{+}}$.

$\therefore \quad \text{n}=|1\quad (-1)\quad (+1)|=2$

3. Salts which react in a manner that only one atom undergoes change in oxidation state but goes in two products with different oxidation state (different than in the reactant) as a result of either oxidation or reduction. 

Let the chemical change be

$\text{A}_{\text{a}}^{+\text{x}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{b}}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}_{\text{c}}^{+\text{y}}\text{D}+\text{A}_{\text{e}}^{+\text{z}}\text{F}$

The number of moles of electrons lost or gained by one mole of ${{\text{A}}_{\text{a}}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{b}}}$ would depend on the fact that how much of $\text{A}$ underwent change to oxidation state $+\text{y}$ and how much of $\text{A}$ underwent change to oxidation state $+z$. n-factor calculation in such cases can be done as follows.

Let us take a chemical change $2\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+4}}+\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$ out of the two moles of $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$, one mole $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$ changes to $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+4}}$ by gaining 3 mole of electrons and the other mole of $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$ changes to $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$ by gaining 5 mole of electrons, so in all 8 mole of electrons are gained by 2 mole of $~{{n}^{+7}}^{+}$. So each mole of $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$ has gained $8/2=4$ mole of electrons. Thus, 4 would be the $\text{n}$-factor on $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$ in this reaction.

If the reaction would have been

$3\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}+\,\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+4}}$

Out of 3 moles of $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$, two moles of $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$ changes to $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+2}}$ by gaining 10 mole of electrons and one mole of $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$ changes to $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+4}}$ by gained 3 mole of electrons. Thus each mole of $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$ have gained 13/3 mole of electrons. Therefore, the $n$-factor of $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{+7}}$ in this reaction would be $13/3$.

"Note that $n$-factor can be a fraction because it is not the number of electrons exchanged but it is the number of moles of electrons exchanged which can be a fraction."

4. Salts that react in a manner that two type of atoms in the salt undergo change in oxidation state (both the atoms are either getting oxidised or reduced). 

Let the change be represented as

$\text{A}_{\text{a}}^{+\text{x}}{{\text{B}}_{\text{b}}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}_{\text{c}}^{+\text{y}}\text{D}+{{\text{E}}_{\text{f}}}{{\text{B}}^{+\text{z}}}$

In such cases, the $n$-factor of the compound would be the sum of individual n-factors of $A$ and $B$.

$\text{n-factor of A}=\,\mid \text{ax}-\text{ ay}|$

$n$-factor of $B=|ax-bz|$ because the total oxidation state of ‘ $b$ ‘ $B$ ‘s in the reactant is – ax (as the total oxidation state of ‘a’ A’s in the reactant is + ax and the total oxidation state of y B’s in the product is bz.

$\therefore \quad n$-factor of ${{A}_{a}}{{B}_{b}}=|ax-ay|+|ax-bz|$

In general, the $n$-factor of the salt will be the total number of mole of electrons lost or gained by one mole of the salt.

For example, we have a reaction, $\text{Cu}_{2}^{+1}~{{\text{S}}^{-2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}+\overset{+4}{\mathop{\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}\,$

in which $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{+}}$and ${{\text{S}}^{2-}}$ both are getting oxidised to $\text{C}{{\text{u}}^{2+}}$ and ${{\text{S}}^{+4}}$ respectively.

$\therefore \quad \text{n-factor of C}{{\text{u}}_{2}}~\text{S}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 2 & (+1)-2 & (+2) \\ \end{array} \right|+\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & (-2)-1 & (+4) \\ \end{array} \right|=8$

5. Salts that react in a manner that two atoms in the salt undergoes change in oxidation state (one atom is getting oxidised and the other is getting reduced). 

The n-factor of such a salt can be calculated either by taking the total number of moles of electrons lost or total number of mole of electrons gained by one mole of the salt.

$\text{K}_{\text{ClO}}^{+5-2}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{KCl}_{3}^{-1}+{{\overset{0}{\mathop{\text{O}}}\,}_{2}}$

In this reaction, ${{\text{O}}^{2-}}$ is getting oxidised to ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{+5}}$ is getting reduced to $\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-1}}$. In each case, 6 mole of electrons are exchanged whether we consider oxidation or reduction.

$\text{n}$-factor of $\text{KCl}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ considering oxidation $=|3(-2)-3(0)|=6$

or $n$-factor of $\text{KCl}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ considering reduction $=11\quad (+5)-1\quad (-1)=6$

6. Salts or compounds which undergoes disproportionation reaction 

Disproportionation reactions are the reactions in which oxidising and reducing agents are same or the same element from the same compound is getting oxidised as well as reduced. n-factor of a disproportionation reaction can only be calculated using a balanced chemical reaction. We will categorize disproportionation reactions into two types.

(a) Disproportionation reactions in which moles of reducing and oxidizing agents are the same. In such compounds we either calculate the $\text{n}$-factor for reducing agent or oxidizing agent since calculating both would be simply a waste of time.

For example, $2{{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\to 2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Out of the 2 mole of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ used in reaction, one mole of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ gets oxidised to ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ (oxidation state of $\text{O}$ changes from -1 to 0 ) while the other mole of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ gets reduced to ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$ (oxidation state of $\text{O}$ changes from -1 to -2 ). When 1 mole of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ gets oxidised to ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$, the half-reaction would be $\text{O}_{2}^{2-}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{O}_{2}^{0}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}$and when 1 mole of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ gets reduced to ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$, the half-reaction would be $\text{O}_{2}^{2-}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}2{{\text{O}}^{2-}}$

Thus, it is evident that one mole of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ (which is either getting oxidised or reduced) will lose or gain 2 mole of electrons. Therefore n-factor of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ as oxidizing as well as reducing agent in this reaction is 2 . Thus

concepts.title: Disproportionation reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Disproportionation reaction

Disproportionation reactions in which moles of compound getting oxidised and reduced are not same i.e. moles of oxidising agent and reducing agent are not same.

For example, $6\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}+12\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}\to 10\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}+2\text{BrO}_{3}^{-}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

In this reaction, the mole of electrons lost by the oxidation of some of the moles of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ are same as the number of mole of electrons gained by the reduction of rest of the moles of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$. Of the 6 moles of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ used, one mole is getting oxidized, loosing 10 electrons (as reducing agent) and 5 moles of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ are getting reduced and accepts 10 moles of electron (as oxidizing agent).

Thus, $n$-factor of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ acting as oxidizing agent is 2 and that $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ acting as reducing agent has $\text{n}-$ factor 10 .

Or when the reaction is written as

$6\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}\to 10\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}}+2\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{+5}}$

where, $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ is not distinguished as how much of it functions as oxidizing agent and how much as reducing agent, then for calculating $n$-factor of compound in such reactions, first find the total number of mole of electrons exchanged (lost or gained) using the balanced equation and divide it with the number of mole of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ involved in the reaction to get the number of mole of electrons exchanged by one mole of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$.

In the overall reaction, the number of mole of electrons exchanged (lost or gained) is 10 and the moles of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ used in the reaction are 6. Thus, each mole of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ has exchanged 10/6 or $5/3$ mole of electrons. Therefore, the $n$-factor of $\text{B}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ when the reaction is written without segregating oxidising and reducing agent is $5/3$.

$\begin{matrix} 6B{{r}_{2}} & \to & 10B{{r}^{-}} & + & 2B{{r}^{+5}} \\ (n=5/3) & {} & (n=1) & {} & (n=5) \\ \end{matrix}$

concepts.title: Law of Equivalence
concepts.conceptBody:
Law of Equivalence and Dilution Effect

Law of Equivalence

It is not practically possible to remember all the chemical reactions in balanced forms though that is how we require them to be for solving problems.

Hence we use the law of equivalence. This law helps us determine the molar ratio of reactants and products. It can be stated as:

According to the law of equivalence, whenever two substances react, the equivalents of one will be equal to the equivalents of other and the equivalents of any product will also be equal to that of the reactant. 

Law of equivalence

For a reaction

$\text{aA}+\text{bB}\to \text{mM}+\text{nN}$

eq. of $A=$ eq. of $B=$ eq. of $M=$ eq. of $N$

or $\quad \frac{\text{ wt}\text{. of }A}{{{E}_{A}}}=\frac{\text{ wt}\text{. of }B}{{{E}_{B}}}=\frac{\text{ wt}\text{. of }M}{{{E}_{M}}}=\frac{\text{ wt}\text{. of }N}{{{E}_{N}}}$

Let us suppose we have a reaction, $A+B\to C+D$. In this reaction, the number of moles of electrons lost by 1 mole of $\text{A}$ are $\text{x}$ and the number of mole of electrons gained by 1 mole of $\text{B}$ are $\text{y}$. Since, the number of mole of electrons lost and gained are not same, the molar ratio in which A & B react cannot be 1: 1. Thus, if we take y moles of $\text{A}$, then the total moles of electrons lost by y moles of A would be ( $\left. \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} x \times y \\ \end{array} \right)$. Similarly, if $x$ moles of B are taken, then the total mole of electrons gained by $x$ moles of $B$ would be $\left( \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} y \times x \\ \end{array} \right)$. Thus, the number of electrons lost by $A$ and number of electrons gained by $B$ becomes equal. For reactant $A$, its $n$-factor is $x$ and the number of moles used are y. So,

The equivalents of $\text{A}$ reacting = moles of $\text{A}$ reacting $\text{xn}$-factor of $\text{A}=\text{y}\times\text{x}$

Similarly, for reactant $\text{B}$, its $\text{n}$-factor is $\text{y}$ and the number of moles used are $\text{x}$. So,

The equivalents of $B$ reacting = moles of $B$ reacting/$n$-factor of $B$ = x/y

Thus, the equivalents of $\text{A}$ reacting would be equal to the equivalents of $\text{B}$ reacting. Thus, the balancing coefficients of the reactant would be as

$\begin{matrix} yA & + & xB & \to & C & + & D \\ (\text{n-f}actor=x) & {} & (\text{n-f}actor=y) & {} & {} & {} & {} \\ \end{matrix}$

The $\text{n}$-factor of $\text{A }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }~\text{B}$ are in the ratio of $\text{x}:\text{y}$, and their molar ratio is $\text{y}:\text{x}$. Thus, molar ratio is inverse of the $\text{n}$-factor ratio.

In general, whenever two substances react with their n-factors in the ratio of a: $b$, then their molar ratio in a balanced chemical reaction would be b : a. 

To get the equivalents of a substance, its $n$-factor is to be known. Let the weight of the substance used in the reaction be $\text{wg}$.

Then, equivalents of substance reacted would be $\frac{\text{w}}{\text{E}}$ or $\frac{\text{w}}{{{\text{M}}_{1}}/\text{n}}=\frac{\text{w}}{{{\text{M}}_{1}}}\times \text{n}$ (where $\text{E}$ and ${{\text{M}}_{1}}$ are the equivalent mass and molar mass of the substance) Thus, in order to calculate the equivalents of a substance, knowledge of $\text{n}$-factor is a must.

When a solution is diluted, the moles and equivalents of solute do not change but molarity and normality change while on taking out a small volume of solution from a larger volume, the molarity and normality of solution do not change but molar and equivalents change proportionately. This concept is useful in solving dilution problems.

In the reaction $\mathrm{I}_2+2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3^{--} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}^{-}+\mathrm{S}_4 \mathrm{O}_6^{--}$ equivalent weight of iodine will be equal to

Assertion : Equivalent weight of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ in the reaction $\mathrm{N}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_3$ is $17 / 3$ while that of $\mathrm{N}_2$ is 28/6.
Reason : Equivalent weight
$=\frac{\text { Molecularweight }}{\text { number of } e^{-l o s t o r} \text { gained }}$

In alkaline medium $\mathrm{ClO}_2$ oxidize $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ in $\mathrm{O}_2$ and reduced itself in $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$then how many mole of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ will oxidize by one mole of $\mathrm{ClO}_2$

$\mathrm{MnO}_4^{2-}$
( 1 mole) in neutral aqueous medium is disproportionate to
